text: I present to you a robotic transgastric GIST resection.
This is a ninety-one-year-old male who presented to the emergency room with GI bleeding.
He required four units of PAC-RED blood cell transfusion.
An endoscopic view reveals a submucosal mass located near the gastric cardia.
The video shows an image of the lesion located near the gastric cardia.
This picture was taken with a robotic scope.
The lesion is visible near the gastroesophageal junction, adjacent to the gastric cardia within the body of the stomach.
The surgeon plans to resect a lesion located high on the posterior wall of the stomach, using three ports placed through the anterior wall for access.

Also, this lesion was fairly close to the GE junction and I felt a transgastric approach would give me the more precise, sorry, the most precise method of resecting this lesion without injury to the GE junction.

The surgeon enters the abdomen to the left of the umbilicus using a five millimeter port with a zero degree laparoscope, followed by the placement of an eight millimeter port in the left lower quadrant. The eight millimeter port is used to pass an SH needle for gastric fixation to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon places a third five millimeter port in the right lower quadrant to introduce a long Dorsey clamp, which is used to occlude the duodenum and minimize the loss of gastric inflation.
The surgeon secures the clamp to the surgical table using a common table clamp, similar to the one used for securing the Nathanson liver retractor.
The surgeon's partner performs an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to inflate the stomach, preparing the surgical field for further intervention.

The surgeon places a single 2-0 Vicryl suture through the mid gastric body and uses a Carter-Thomason device to pull out both ends of the suture, securing the stomach to the abdominal wall to provide counter traction during port placement.

In hindsight, I did not put enough of these gastric fixation sutures. I had a lot of difficulty during port placement as the stomach was very elastic and did not allow me to puncture it very easily without good counter tension.
In the future, I probably would put three or four different sutures to fixate the stomach in order to provide good counter tension during port insertion.
The surgeon uses a local anesthetic needle to verify the planned trajectory for port placement, ensuring alignment with the inflated stomach secured by sutures.
The surgeon inserts a needle three times to place transgastric ports, beginning with the first port in the left upper quadrant.

In hindsight, I probably should have made a small gastrotomy with a hook or something like that in order to make it easier to puncture the stomach. The stomach, despite full insufflation with an EGT scope, was still very elastic and I had a hard time puncturing the stomach fully and fully inserting the port.
As I mentioned in hindsight, I should have put more gastric fixation sutures to provide more counter tension during the port insertion.
The surgeon uses an EGD (esophagogastroduodenoscopy) to guide the insertion of the port, ensuring it is fully inserted into the stomach.
The surgeon attempts to push the port further into the stomach, with a significant portion still visible outside the gastric wall. After considerable effort, the port is successfully inserted.
The surgeon places the second port into the abdominal wall. During this process, the stomach is pushed away, causing the first port in the left upper quadrant to fall out.

The surgeon refines the gastrotomy and reinserts the first port, visually searching for the gastrotomy site from the initial port placement. The stomach is observed with four holes by the end of the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to reposition the first port back into the stomach, potentially creating an additional gastrotomy during this maneuver.
The surgeon successfully inserts the third port into the stomach without much difficulty. The three ports are visibly spaced approximately five centimeters apart from each other.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a vessel sealer in the right hand. The lesion near the gastroesophageal junction is visible and appears pedunculated.
The surgeon observes the movement of the lesion, noting that it appears to arise from the muscularis layer in the superior portion of its attachment to the stomach.
The surgeon performs the resection of the lesion using a vessel sealer, with the process appearing smooth and controlled.

The surgeon performs a full thickness resection of the lesion at its superior attachment to the stomach. Initially, the mucosa around the inferior portion of the lesion is divided, with the operative site appearing stable throughout the procedure.

The laparoscopic insufflator provides insufflation through one of the robotic ports, while the endoscopic gastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is visible but not actively used, aiding in the identification of the gastroesophageal (GE) junction.

An external Dorsey clamp is applied to the duodenum to maintain gastric insufflation. The surgeon performs a full thickness resection of the lesion, resulting in a gastrotomy on the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon experiences a loss of gastric insufflation as CO₂ escapes from the gastric body into the abdominal cavity, resulting in a complete loss of the working space.
The surgeon opens a laparoscopic port and attaches it to a striker smoke evacuator, allowing the abdomen to be redistended with full gastric insufflation through the robotic port.

The surgeon completes the final part of the resection of the gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) using articulated robotic instruments. The vessel sealer is maneuvered at various angles to access the lesion within the confined surgical space.

Having done these laparoscopically, I can tell you ergonomically this operation was much better robotically as I did not have to contort my body in order to move the instruments to the location I needed.
The surgeon uses robotic assistance to suture the posterior gastrotomy created after the complete resection of the lesion in the stomach wall.
The video shows the gradual decrease in gastric distension, with the gastric rugae becoming more prominent as the distension is lost.

Initially I panicked and then I realized I could just open up the abdominal ports to desufflate the abdomen and allow the gastric insufflation through the robotic ports to catch up and help to send the stomach.
The surgeon uses a 90-day V-Loc suture, six inches in length, to perform a single-layer, full-thickness closure of the gastric defect after regaining insufflation.
The robotic platform facilitates the closure of the gastric defect, with the EGD in place to identify the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, ensuring the closure does not encroach upon it.

The surgeon completes the closure of the gastric defect using a V-lock suture, running it back two throws to secure the closure. During this process, a small blood vessel is inadvertently pierced, causing minor bleeding.
Fortunately that didn't lead to any significant complications during this operation.
The surgeon prepares to remove a lesion measuring 5 by 5 by 3 centimeters, assessing its size as suitable for transoral removal.
The surgeon uses an eight millimeter laparoscopic endocatch to place the specimen into a retrieval bag. The specimen is then removed transorally with a small biopsy forcep through the EGD.

The surgeon inspects the closure site for any gaps, noting a small amount of blood at the gastroesophageal (GE) junction. Gastric distension is observed to decrease once the endoscope is removed from the mouth.

The surgeon's partner reinserts the scope to re-encephalate the stomach, while the surgeon uses a robotic suction irrigator to remove gastric fluid. This allows for a clearer evaluation of the defect closure and an attempt to locate the bleeding point in the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies a small mucosal defect inferiorly and places a 3-0 Vicryl figure-of-eight suture to ensure the mucosal edges are well opposed.

A small tear is visible in the anterior portion of the gastroesophageal junction, with no active bleeding observed through endoscopy and enterogastric view. A small clot adheres to the area and is left in place.
The patient did not have any issues with bleeding post-op.
Three ports are withdrawn from the stomach, leaving holes that are closed with figure-of-eight vicryl sutures. The visual focus is on the suturing technique used to close the stomach openings.
Thank you for watching.
